8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control that contain any part of the other party's Confidential Information; or
8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to de stroy by shredding or incineration all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have
perturbation.changed_text:
8.2 Each party undertakes within 3 days of receipt of a written request of the other party:
8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control that contain any part of the other party's Confidential Information; or
8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to de stroy by shredding or incineration all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the timeframe for returning or destroying confidential information to 3 days could be problematic.  While there isn't a universally mandated timeframe, such a short period may be deemed commercially unreasonable, especially considering the location of the contract is the Netherlands.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code requires contracts to be executed in good faith and in a reasonable manner. A 3-day timeframe for returning or destroying confidential information, upon written request, could be deemed unreasonable, especially if the volume of information is substantial or requires complex retrieval or secure destruction processes. This may violate the principle of reasonableness embedded in Dutch contract law.

This article necessitates that contracts be executed in good faith and reasonably. The Perturbation Explanation also states such a short period may be deemed commercially unreasonable. perturbation.original_text:
8.  TERM AND TERMINATION
8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect for a period of 2 years.
perturbation.changed_text:
8.  TERM AND TERMINATION
8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect for a period of 10 years.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the term of the NDA to 10 years could be considered unreasonable or excessively restrictive under Dutch law, potentially conflicting with principles of fair competition and freedom of contract, especially if the confidential information becomes outdated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code states that a contract should be executed in a reasonable manner. A 10-year NDA may be seen as an unreasonably long duration, particularly if the type of confidential information exchanged has a short lifespan. The reasonableness of a contract term is assessed by considering various circumstances, and a long NDA can unduly restrict trade. Therefore, extending the NDA to 10 years may violate Dutch law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the NDA term to 10 years, which the law explanation suggests may violate Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code. This article implies contracts should be executed reasonably, and a 10-year NDA might be unreasonably long, potentially restricting trade, especially if the confidential information's lifespan is short. The perturbation explanation supports this by noting the potential conflict with fair competition principles under Dutch law if the confidential information becomes outdated. The law snippets do not provide sufficient context, but based on the generated Law explanation, the extended term could be deemed unreasonable under Dutch law.

perturbation.original_text:
3. KEEPING CONFIDENT IAL INFORMATION C ONFIDENTIAL
Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise no lesser security measures and degree of care than those which it applies to its own confidential information and which it warrants as providing adequate protection against any unauthorised disclosure, copying or use.
perturbation.changed_text:
3. KEEPING CONFIDENT IAL INFORMATION C ONFIDENTIAL
Each party shall in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party exercise security measures it deems reasonable in its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the standard of care to 'security measures it deems reasonable in its sole discretion' provides significantly less protection than the original language. This may be problematic under Dutch contract law, potentially conflicting with the principle of good faith and reasonableness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6:248 of the Dutch Civil Code requires that contracts be performed in good faith. Changing the security measures to the party's sole discretion might be seen as a violation of good faith, particularly if the measures implemented are demonstrably inadequate. By changing the text, the contract allows a party to potentially avoid taking reasonable measures to protect Confidential Information and thus may violate Dutch law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires each party to exercise security measures no lesser than those applied to its own confidential information, ensuring adequate protection against unauthorized disclosure. The changed text replaces this with 'security measures it deems reasonable in its sole discretion.' This significant weakening of the standard of care contradicts the principle of good faith, potentially violating Dutch law as the party can implement demonstrably inadequate measures, as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text:
11. GOVERNING LAW AND JU RISDICTION
This Agreement and any non -contractual obligations arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of The Netherlands and the competent court in The Hague, The Netherlands shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise from or in connection with it.
perturbation.changed_text:
11. GOVERNING LAW AND JU RISDICTION
This Agreement and any non -contractual obligations arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of Delaware, USA and the competent court in The Hague, The Netherlands shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise from or in connection with it.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, USA, while maintaining jurisdiction in The Hague creates a conflict. Dutch courts may need to apply Delaware law, potentially leading to complications and increased costs, especially if Delaware law conflicts with Dutch public policy.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 3 of Rome I Regulation (Regulation (EC) No 593/2008)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Rome I Regulation (Article 3) generally allows parties to choose the governing law, the choice must be bona fide and not for the purposes of evading mandatory rules of the jurisdiction where the contract is performed. The change is designed to possibly bring a US court system into play, which may not be available. Dutch courts, in a contract between Dutch parties, where work is being done in The Netherlands will raise suspicion if Delaware law is chosen to avoid the Dutch Civil Code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Verbraucherkreditgesetz - German Consumer Credit Law | Trans-Lex.org This page uses so called "cookies" to improve its service (i.e. "tracking"). Learn more and opt out of tracking I agree Principles 1672 Bibliography 1035 Materials 86 Archive 66 History & Modern Evolution of Transnational Commercial Law Verbraucherkreditgesetz - German Consumer Credit Law Document-Id: 600500, Please cite as: "https://www.trans-lex.org/600500" Title Verbraucherkreditgesetz - German Consumer Credit Law Table of Contents Verbraucherkreditgesetz § 11 Verzugszinsen, Anrechnung von Teilleistungen Content Verbraucherkreditgesetz § 11 Verzugszinsen, Anrechnung von Teilleistungen (1) Soweit der Verbraucher mit Zahlungen, die er auf Grund des Kreditvertrags schuldet, in Verzug kommt, ist der geschuldete Vertrag mit fünf vom Hundert über dem jeweiligen Diskontsatz der Deutschen Bundesbank zu verzinsen, wenn nicht im Einzelfall der Kreditgeber einen höheren oder der Verbraucher einen niedrigeren Schaden nachweist. (2) Nach Eintritt des Verzugs anfallende Zinsen sind auf einem gesonderten Konto zu verbuchen und dürfen nicht in ein Kontokorrent mit dem geschuldeten Betrag oder anderen Forderungen des Kreditgebers eingestellt werden. Hinsichtlich dieser Zinsen gilt § 289 Satz des Bürgerlichen Gesetzbuchs mit der Maßgabe, dass der Kreditgeber Schadensersatz nur bis zur Höhe des gesetzlichen Zinssatzes verlangen kann. (3) Zahlungen des Verbrauchers, die zur Zahlung der gesamten fälligen Schuld nicht ausreichen, werden abweichend von § 367 Abs. 1 des Bürgerlichen Gesetzbuchs zunächst auf die Kosten der Rechtsverfolgung, dann auf den übrigen geschuldeten Betrag (Absatz 1) und zuletzt auf die Zinsen (Absatz 2) angerechnet. Der Kreditgeber darf Teilzahlungen nicht zurückweisen. Auf die Ansprüche auf Zinsen finden die §§ 197 und 218 Abs. 2 des Bürgerlichen Gesetzbuchs keine Anwendung. Die Sätze 1 bis 3 sind nicht anzuwenden, soweit Zahlungen auf Vollstreckungstitel geleistet werden, deren Hauptforderung auf Zinsen lautet. Referring Principles Trans-Lex Principle: VII.7 - Right to charge compound interest How to search - Contact - Team - Trustees - Disclaimer / Legal information / Privacy - Twitter - Facebook A project of CENTRAL , University of Cologne.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies the laws of The Netherlands as governing law, and the changed contract replaces this with Delaware, USA law while keeping the jurisdiction in The Hague, Netherlands. The Law Explanation states that the choice of governing law must be bona fide and not for the purpose of evading mandatory rules of the jurisdiction where the contract is performed, raising suspicion if Delaware law is chosen to avoid the Dutch Civil Code. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references German Consumer Credit Law, and though not directly applicable, it hints at legal frameworks that protect consumers. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states this creates a conflict as Dutch courts may need to apply Delaware law, potentially leading to complications and increased costs. This situation is an Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction, because it sets a requirement that violates legal principles, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.
